title: THE FOUR OLDS audio play — sc03 the barn net

lines.sfx: Wind pulls along the barn boards in long draws, and the seams tick where the cold works through.
A breaker snaps over, then a second, then a third, and the trainer's hum rises under the room and settles.
The percolator catches on the hot plate and starts a slow, uneven knock.
Out on the grade, gravel turns under tires as one truck climbs toward the barn.
The engine dies, a door slams, and boots come across the plank floor.

lines.text: Evening.
Gunny.
Percolator's already going. You slept out here again.
Coffee's coffee.

lines.sfx: A second truck comes up the gravel, idles a moment, and shuts off, and another door slams.

lines.text: (approaching) Cold enough for you? My hangar door froze halfway down and I just left it froze. Figured I'd get to it when it thaws, or maybe I won't.
Evening, Stitch.
Cricket. Gunny. That wind's coming straight down out of Canada and nobody asked it to.
Wipe your damn boots. I swept in here.

lines.sfx: A third truck rolls in slow, the engine ticking as it cools, and a door thumps shut.

lines.text: (approaching, off) Did anybody touch the trainer before I got the temperature logged?
Nobody touched your trainer, Dutch.
Evening, Cricket. It reads fifty-one degrees in here. I'll write down fifty-one.
There's a cup by the breakers if you want it.
Alright. We're past the hour again.
The hour's a suggestion, Gunny. We got here when we got here.
Headsets. Both of you. Dutch, you too.

lines.sfx: Chairs drag on the plank floor, and four headset cans settle over four heads with the soft double click of the jacks going in.
A thin carrier hiss opens up under the room and rides beneath the filtered voices.

lines.text: (into radio) Net check. Gunny, loud and clear.
(into radio) Got you loud, Gunny.
Reading you.
I've got the loop.
(into radio) Apollo Eighteen. Lunar descent rehearsal. Number two thousand, eight hundred and six.
(room, to Cricket) He says the whole number every week like there's a stenographer under the workbench.
(into radio) Say again for the loop.
I didn't key up, Gunny.
(into radio) Then key up. If it's not on the loop it didn't happen.
(into radio, dry) Backup pilot's on the loop and just tickled about it.
(into radio) Copy tickled.
(room) Two thousand eight hundred and change. Call it three thousand Tuesdays and be done, Cricket. Nobody's out here counting the pennies but Dutch.
(room) I'm counting the pennies.
(room) Two thousand, eight hundred and six.
(into radio) Program's loaded. Starting the clock. Ten thousand feet.
(room) You see that landing come down Sunday? On the set at the diner?
(room) I saw it.
(room) We all saw it. Marge had it up over the pie case with the sound turned off, which was a mercy.
(room) Long. He floated it. Sitting up there hunting for a flat spot with the fuel light already on him.
(into radio) Eight thousand feet. Coming down at ninety.
(room) He came in eleven seconds long on the braking burn. I wrote it on a napkin at the diner and I wrote it again at the house to be sure of the napkin.
(room) Eleven. See, I'm not making it up.
(room, dry) Eleven and a half. I rounded the half off for Marge.
(room) You come in long on the boat, you put the reactor in somebody's front room.
(room) And that fella's got a patch on his arm and a parade waiting on him in Houston.
(into radio) Six thousand. Down at eighty, holding it.
(room) Not one of those boys would have passed my physical. I'd have washed him out on the eyes before he sat down.
(room) You'd have washed him out in the parking lot before he ever got near the eye chart.
(room, dry) I'd have been fair about it.

lines.sfx: Under the room the trainer's hum sags a quarter step and thins out.
A buzz starts up beneath the hum, catching and dropping and catching again.

lines.text: (into radio) Caution. Bus B.
(into radio) Say again. Bus B?
(into radio) Bus B. I'm calling the walk.
(room) There she goes again. Every damn Tuesday, right on the dot.
(room, not moving) That relay went in the spring of 'seventy-six. April. It's been doing this since Carter was running for the job.
(room) Steady, or does it stutter?

lines.sfx: The buzz breaks up ragged, almost clears, then catches hard again.

lines.text: (room) Stutters.

lines.sfx: A page turns, slow, under the next voice.

lines.text: (room) Kick it. Low left corner.

lines.sfx: A boot comes down flat on sheet metal, one hard bang.
The buzz cuts out, and the hum climbs back up full and holds clean.

lines.text: (room, dry) Same corner as last time.
(room) You didn't even look up off your page.
(room) No.
(into radio) Bus B corrected. Back on the card. Four thousand feet.
(room) See, Sunday's boy, right about here's where he starts sweating through the patch.
(room) Loop, Stitch.
(into radio) I said the man was sweating.
(into radio) Copy sweating.
(room) One of these Tuesdays that trainer's gonna quit for good and take April 'seventy-six down with it.
(room) We'll get it done.
(into radio) Two thousand feet. Coming down at fifty.
(into radio) One thousand. Looking good.
(room) And that's the thing about Sunday, is he had the gas, he had plenty of gas, he just wouldn't trust it—
(into radio) Seven hundred feet.
(into radio) Five hundred. Down at twenty.

lines.sfx: The room goes quiet, and the percolator gives one last knock and settles off in the corner.
The hum holds alone, and four men breathe under it.

lines.text: (into radio) Three hundred feet.

lines.sfx: Nothing keys up, and the hum runs on by itself for one long beat.

lines.text: (into radio) Contact light. Engine stop.

lines.sfx: The hum holds alone through another long beat, and no one on the loop says a word.

lines.text: (room) You were down at three feet a second with fuel still on the gauge. That's the number.
(room) Sunday's boy doesn't see three feet a second in his sleep.
(room) That's how it's flown.
(room) It's just the card.
(into radio) That's the run. Net closed. Same time Tuesday.
(into radio) God willing.

lines.sfx: A transmit key clicks twice on the loop, and two clicks answer it back from across the barn.
The headset jacks pull free one after another, and the carrier hiss drops out of the room.
A pen scratches across a log page.

lines.text: (low, to himself) T plus two thousand eight hundred six. Bus B corrected. Crew present.
Well. That's me. Got a froze door at home to go stand and look at.
Mud at the door on your way out, Stitch.
I'll get it Tuesday.
Fifty-one when we started. I'm logging forty-nine.
Night, Dutch.
Cricket.
Same road Tuesday.
Gunny.

lines.sfx: Chairs scrape back from the bench, and three sets of boots go out across the planks toward the door.
Outside, one truck turns over and catches, then a second, then a third, each pulling away down the grade.
The gravel sound thins as the trucks drop off the property one by one, and the last of it goes under the wind.
Inside, the breakers go over one at a time, and the trainer's hum steps down and quits.
A hasp rattles, and a padlock pulls shut with one hard click.
Boots cross the yard, climb two porch steps, and a screen door claps once and settles.
Wind works the barn boards alone, and nothing else moves on the property.
